schools.summary: Garden = unnamed Gethsemane; cheimarrous = winter torrent; John omits the agony already told by the Synoptics.
Entering a place known to Judas proves a voluntary sacrifice; without obedience no atonement.
Garden typology: sin begun in a garden is blotted out in a garden; Christ drinks of the brook of his Passion.
He journeys at midnight, hastening to the known place to spare his plotters and show willing death.
Captain of salvation takes the field; David's flight over Kidron and Ps 110:7 as Messianic type.
A friend's garden for secret devotion and to avoid a tumultuous rescue.

debates.question: Is 'Kedron' the brook 'of the cedars' or the indeclinable Hebrew 'Kidron' (= black)?

sides.position: 'Kidron', indeclinable, from a root meaning black/dark
'of the cedars' (tōn Kedrōn)
holders: rejected as a copyist error / Arabic reading

schools.summary: Judas knew the place as Jesus' habitual night resort for prayer (Passover crowding pushed him from the city).
Knowing the place shows Christ went to meet, not hide from, the traitor.

outliers.note: Judas's plan to seize Jesus by night and alone at his prayer-place is 'the very spirit of the devil.'

schools.summary: speira = Roman cohort from Antonia, distinct from the temple 'officers'; full-moon torches betray a determined hunt.
The band was Roman, granted by the governor with authority to crush resistance.
Soldiers persuaded 'by hiring them'; torches lest Christ escape in the dark.
So large a retinue is the trembling of a bad conscience; the priests' men the most enraged.

sides.position: uncertain; no fixed number can be determined
a very large force, perhaps 500+

outliers.note: Adds rabbinic color: the torch-dance of the feast of Tabernacles.

schools.summary: Foreknowledge (cf. Jn 13:1) means he was not surprised; going forth to meet them is a voluntary act; the kiss falls here.
His omniscience is deity; by a single word he shows enemies had only the power he permitted.
'Jesus of Nazareth' = the common, faintly contemptuous official title; their eyes were holden; the kiss precedes.
'I am he' (egō eimi) is the divine Name (Exod 3:14) and an arrow at the traitor's conscience.

debates.question: What is the force of 'I am he' (egō eimi)?

sides.position: the divine Name 'I AM' (Exod 3:14; Jn 8:58)
a plain, honest self-identification refusing to equivocate

outliers.note: His perfect tense gives a vivid picture of Judas 'in the very act' of betraying Jesus.

schools.summary: A word prostrates the armed crowd: God hidden in flesh; elect fall forward in repentance, reprobate fall backward.
A foretaste of judgment: the Lamb lays enemies low by his voice (Isa 11:4; 2 Thess 2:8).
The terror is the majesty of his person and stricken conscience, not necessarily a miracle.

debates.question: Was the falling caused by a miracle or by the majesty of Christ / conscience?

sides.position: a true display of divine power
the moral majesty of his person and the divine Name; no proof of miracle
left open

outliers.note: Their note here is mis-attached, commenting on the Samaritan woman rather than this verse.

schools.summary: The men rise unhurt yet keep seeking him: judicial hardness; miracles save none without efficacious grace.
Their fear has passed; Christ's repeated question still aims to free the disciples; the band stand overawed.

outliers.note: The reprobate, struck with blindness and given to Satan (Rom 1:28), become 'more stupid than brute beasts.'

schools.summary: Captain of salvation goes foremost; words of authority secure the disciples (cf. Lk 22:31).
The Good Shepherd goes forward to meet the wolves and guard his flock, a rule for all shepherds.
His power over men's spirits kept them from seizing even Peter.

outliers.note: Uses the verse polemically against the heretic Mani, who fled prison instead of giving himself like Christ or Jonah.

schools.summary: The fulfilment formula (hina plērōthē) puts Jesus' saying on a par with Scripture; John quotes the substance of Jn 17:12.
Temporal sparing serves eternal preservation; Christ never tries his people beyond the strength he has given.

debates.question: Does the 'saying' (Jn 17:12) concern spiritual or bodily preservation?

sides.position: spoken of spiritual safety, here applied to temporal rescue
the bodily rescue is the outward edge of an eternal keeping

schools.summary: John alone names Peter and Malchus (both dead by his day); the 'right ear'; the diminutive ōtarion; the sword of Lk 22:38.
Where got the sword? Defending his Master, not avenging self; the healing teaches good to enemies.
Peter's zeal is condemned: courageous but unlawful, resisting God-appointed power and contrary to God's word.
Allegory: Malchus ('about to reign'); the ear healed = new hearing in the Spirit, bondage exchanged for liberty.

debates.question: Where did Peter get a sword?

sides.position: foresaw the occasion and provided one
kept the knife from the Passover lamb
one of the two swords the eleven carried (Lk 22:38)

outliers.note: Reads Malchus's name ('about to reign') and the healed ear as the abolition of the old hearing and the gift of the Spirit's new hearing.

schools.summary: 'Cup' = metaphor for Christ's death (Mt 20:22; 26:39); the only such image in John; an echo of the unrecorded Gethsemane prayer.
A private man may not resist public authority (Mt 26:52); afflictions are the Father's medicinal draughts, drunk within the bounds of God's word.
The 'captain' = Roman chiliarch/tribune now present (cf. Acts 21:31), distinct from the temple officers; bound with no charge in law.
Bound by those who should have wished to be loosed; the 'high priest that year' shows the office sold to ambitious men.
Led away in triumph 'as if raising a trophy'; even enemies echo the truth of Jn 11:50.
The Son's body was bound that our souls might be loosed from sin and Satan.

outliers.note: Note here is mis-attached, commenting on Christ's hunger and the Samaritan woman.

schools.summary: Annas the sagan/elder, ex-high priest with five priestly sons, taken first as experienced; the office now near-annual under Rome.
The high priesthood, by Law for life, was corrupted by Roman governors deposing and installing for money or favor.
Led as a lamb to the slaughter through the sheep-gate (Neh 3:1); providence set a bad man in the chair for the deed.
Judas earns damnation for the willfulness of his treachery, no praise for its usefulness.

outliers.note: Reads Annas inspecting sacrifices for blemish as a type: Christ the spotless sacrifice presented and approved for the altar.

schools.summary: Recalling Caiaphas's counsel (Jn 11:50) shows he had prejudged the case; an improper judge.
God used the wicked high priest's mouth to utter a true prophecy, as he turned Balaam's curse to blessing (Num 23).
Henry: the recalled counsel shows Caiaphas a bad man and yet testifies, from an enemy, to Christ's innocence.
Debate over the 'other disciple'; Peter followed afar; possible transposition of v.24 here.
To deny being Christ's disciple is to deny Christ himself; had Peter died then he would have been lost.
John omits his own name from humility; Providence let Peter fall first so he might pity sinners.
Peter undertook a duty Christ had released him from; better to have prayed in a corner than enter.

debates.question: Who is the 'other disciple' known to the high priest?

sides.position: the apostle John himself
not John (a fisherman) but a Jerusalem disciple / the supper's host
undeterminable; Scripture is silent

outliers.note: 'The fire of love was smothered in Peter's breast' as he warmed himself at the persecutors' coals.

schools.summary: Peter at the door of the court; the door-keeper (thyrōros) is female, as Jewish custom and Josephus attest.
The difficulty of entry was a providential hint Peter failed to take: he stood at the door of temptation.
Henry: the other disciple's kindness in bringing Peter in proved a snare, letting him into temptation.
The maid's question (mē kai su) expects 'no'; no real charge, mere recognition, yet Peter dreads it (he had wounded a servant).
A feeble woman's voice overthrows him: human strength is smoke; an evasive denial is still a denial.
Henry: so slight a test shames Peter; the fear of man brings a snare.
anthrakian ('coals', only here & 21:9) echoes Peter's restoration; servants vs officers; cold nights at Passover; soldiers withdrawn.
Peter's stupidity in standing with the wicked after denying his Master, perhaps held by fear of fresh danger outside.
Henry: warming with evil-doers, men grow cold toward good; gather not my soul with sinners.

outliers.note: Note mis-attached, commenting on Christ's hunger rather than the fire-scene.

schools.summary: Questions on disciples (sedition) and doctrine (heresy/treason) aim to extort a charge; Annas's preliminary exam; night trial is illegal.
Annas seeks to brand Jesus a factious leader and an enemy of God, not to learn the truth.
He interrogates Christ as a seditious man and false prophet; Christ reproves his impudence.

outliers.note: Resolves 'in secret have I said nothing' with Christ's private teaching: he spoke openly in that many heard, though many did not understand.

schools.summary: parrēsia: taught openly in synagogue and temple (Jn 7:14; 8:20; 6:59); nothing taught privately he had not taught in public (Mt 10:27).
The verse rebukes Caiaphas, not private exposition; the substance of his doctrine was always the same, the manner various.
Henry: Christ's doctrine, plainly preached, need not be ashamed before the most numerous assembly.

outliers.note: Takes 'the world' here to mean the Jewish people only, since Christ did not preach to the Gentiles.

schools.summary: Stress on 'why' not 'me'; appeal to abundant witnesses (Mk 12:37) and to the rule that defence witnesses be heard first; insist on justice (1 Pet 4).
Surprise that the bound defendant should be made to accuse himself.
Henry: the appeal tacitly charges the judges' illegality; Christ's doctrine may safely appeal to all who know it.
Fulfils 'I gave my cheek to the smiters' (Isa 50:6); turning the cheek is preparation of heart, not bodily posture; Christ did both.
rhapisma (rod or, likelier, an insulting slap); an unauthorised blow proves this was no legal trial (cf. Acts 23:2).
Unable to disprove or admit his words, the priests reveal it is 'not a trial but a faction, a tyranny.'
A barbarous assembly, judges 'as cruel as ferocious beasts,' void of justice and modesty.

debates.question: Was the blow with a rod or with the open hand?

sides.position: uncertain; likely an insulting slap in the face
possibly a stroke with a rod or staff of office

outliers.note: Note attached here is a stray catechetical fragment on the 'Catholic Church,' unrelated to the verse.

schools.summary: A lawful demand for evidence; Mt 5:39 refers to private matters, not judicial, and allows insisting on justice (1 Pet 2:19-23).
Christian patience = bear the injury and renounce revenge, yet one may expostulate without rancour (Rom 12:21).

debates.question: Does Christ here break his own rule to 'turn the other cheek' (Mt 5:39)?

sides.position: no; the precept governs private revenge, not a just demand for evidence
he notably did not turn the other cheek, but answered for argument's sake

schools.summary: Aorist 'sent' (not pluperfect): Annas's preliminary exam over, he then sent the still-bound Jesus to Caiaphas.
Read as a pluperfect parenthesis ('had sent') belonging after v.13; a recapitulation.

debates.question: Is the verb an aorist ('sent') or a pluperfect ('had sent', parenthesis)?

sides.position: pluperfect parenthesis belonging after v.13
plain aorist: sent after the Annas examination

outliers.note: Note mis-attached, on the Samaritans of John 4.

schools.summary: Repeated periphrastic imperfects (cf. v.18); denial interleaved with the examination; the three denials cannot be exactly paralleled.
Harmonizes the Evangelists' accounts; the Physician's prophecy fulfilled against the sick man's presumption.
The once-fervid disciple now torpid: a warning against trusting in self.
Peter hardens himself by indulgence in sin; be early on guard.

outliers.note: Allegory: denials/cock-crowings = denials of Christ's deity and the two resurrections; damsels = lust and carnal delight; servants = the devils.

schools.summary: The kinsman's question (ouk) expects 'yes'; Peter, observed by Malchus's relative, is in dire peril of arrest; Jesus turns and looks (Lk 22:61).
Full harmony of the three denials by place and time; the disputed 'therefore' of v.24; 'a cock crew.'
He swore from fear of prosecution for the ear; no sign avails until Christ casts his eyes on him (Lk 22:61).
Henry: the deepening fall shows sin's deceitfulness; lying is a fruitful sin needing lie upon lie.
'Passover' = the Chagigah/festival (not the lamb already eaten), keeping John in harmony with the Synoptics; night trial illegal; Sanhedrin lacks power of death.
O impious blindness: fearing defilement by the praetorium while not fearing to shed innocent blood; lamb of the 14th vs festival of the 15th.
Two faults of hypocrisy: inward pollution ignored for outward; men's traditions preferred to God's commandments (Titus 1:15; Mt 23:24).
They strained at a gnat and swallowed a camel; hurried the trial early to forestall the people.
Chrysostom: Christ kept the passover a day before; the night's examination proved nothing, so he was sent to Pilate.

debates.question: What is 'the passover' they wished to eat?

sides.position: the festival/Chagigah of the 15th, the lamb already eaten the night before
the paschal lamb; Christ ate early or died at the hour of its slaying

schools.summary: Pilate goes outside to the gallery; demands the formal charge (katēgoria) without which the trial cannot proceed.
The heathen indulges a superstition he despises, yet plays the good judge in requiring an accusation.
Henry: even-handedly credits Pilate's diligence, condescension, and demand for an accusation (no man condemned unheard).
No charge brought; they claim their verdict suffices; an insolent 'pose of infallibility'; Pilate retries it anyway, knowing their envy (Mt 27:18).
Pride intoxicates them; against the facts of his healings and raisings they brand the benefactor a malefactor.
Henry: the priests rude, spiteful, and proud, branding the Benefactor a malefactor.

outliers.note: Note mis-attached, on the nobleman of John 4.

schools.summary: Pilate ironically returns the case; the Jews had lost capital power (Archelaus deposed, A.D. 6-7; 'forty years before' in the Talmud); they verbally yield it but make Pilate their executioner.
Pilate reproaches their lawless rush: is it the equity of their Law to condemn without a crime?

debates.question: Had the Jews retained any power of capital punishment?

sides.position: yes in purely religious cases (blasphemy/stoning), but not for sedition/treason
they had wholly lost it; they only claim it to make Pilate execute their verdict

outliers.note: Note here is a stray catechetical fragment on the Holy Spirit and the apostles, unrelated to the verse.

schools.summary: Crucifixion is Roman, not Jewish (which would have stoned for blasphemy); so Roman execution fulfilled the lifting-up sayings (Jn 3:14; 12:32).
Read the Passion by the eternal purpose of God: Christ's condemnation blots out ours, so we glory even in his ignominy.
Henry: those who designed to defeat Christ's sayings were overruled to fulfil them; crucifixion made him a curse for us (Gal 3:13).
A private examination; the kingship question is the vital charge (Lk 23:2 alone gives the accusation); emphatic 'Thou' — a spiritual king, not Caesar's rival.
Pilate takes Jesus apart suspecting innocence; the Lord knew the exchange but willed it written for our sakes.
With no charge brought, Pilate examines Jesus himself about the rumored kingship.
Pilate's laborious inquiry whether Christ was justly accused, taking up the most offensive charge first.
The counter-question distinguishes the political 'King of the Jews' (Pilate's sense) from the theocratic (a Jew's sense), and demands fair process; sincere inquiry or Sanhedrin trap?
Henry: the counter-question summons Pilate to weigh who accuses and why.
'Am I a Jew?' expresses proud Roman scorn; the charge comes from 'thine own nation'; the blunt 'What hast thou done?' presses for the real crime.
Henry: Pilate's scorn testifies to the nation's disgrace; good names suffer for bad men who wear them.
'Of this world' = nature/origin, not extent; proof he is no political king is that his servants do not fight (cf. v.10); Eusebius on the 'heavenly' kingdom.
A heavenly kingdom is perpetual; princes may only 'accidentally' defend it, for it is founded on the Spirit and grows by martyrs' blood (Lk 17:21).
'Not from hence,' not 'not here': his kingdom is in the world (tares among wheat) yet a stranger to it.

outliers.note: Note mis-attached, commenting on the nobleman's faith in John 4.

schools.summary: 'Thou sayest' = yes; born/came for this; the kingdom is the reign of truth, ruling minds without force; the 'good confession' (1 Tim 6:13).
'Every one of the truth hears my voice' is grace: he hears because he is of the truth, not the reverse.
Silent under the charge, Christ speaks freely of his kingdom, instructing Pilate and drawing him higher.
A solemn affirmation that to teach truth is Christ's peculiar office; trusting him we cannot be deceived.
'What is truth?' likely asked in contempt/irritation, not awaiting an answer; Pilate's verdict 'no fault' should have freed Jesus (Lk 23:4).
An expression of disdain by an insulted, proud Pilate, yet even he is 'somewhat moved' by the gospel's efficacy.
Pilate goes out at once, recalling the Passover custom by which he hoped to save Jesus.
Henry: the acquittal honours Christ's innocence and unfolds his death as sacrifice for our sins.

debates.question: In what spirit did Pilate ask 'What is truth?'

sides.position: contempt / jesting / irritation, not awaiting an answer
disdain at being thought ignorant
the earnest inquirer of the older interpreters

outliers.note: Note here is a stray catechetical fragment on the Holy Spirit, unrelated to the verse.

schools.summary: A custom, not a law, of unknown origin (all four Gospels); 'the king of the Jews' said in irony, mocking the people's subjection.
Pilate's middle path; the custom itself a profanation, acquitting the guilty being abomination (Prov 17:15).
Henry: Pilate trims to please all sides, governed by worldly wisdom rather than equity.
Barabbas a robber/insurgent/murderer (Lk 23:19); priests stir the crowd; 'son of a father'; Jesus dies on the cross planned for Barabbas.
A providence: the Son of God should not be ransomed by so dishonorable a price; the guilty go free, the innocent bears all sin.
Henry: the crowd cries Christ down in heat; sin, a robber, is foolishly chosen over Christ who would enrich us.

outliers.note: Appends a long excursus here on how and when the Jews lost the power of capital judgment.

themes: robber preferred to the Prince of peace
